Yeah this does need to happen after object resolution in order to preserve the existing local shuffling semantics, which I think we want to do.

I'd vote for locking around the existing `ShufflingBatcher` abstraction or some sensible port, which is already optimized to efficiently interleave additions to the buffer and popping of shuffled batches: it will cheaply add blocks to the buffer until the minimum buffer size is met and the consumer requests a batch, it will then generate shuffle indices once for the enter buffer, and yield shuffled batches via those shuffle indices, making runs of block additions and runs of batch pops efficient.

This is a natural merge/sync point for the data-parallel threadpool anyway, since we need to fill the shuffle buffer as fast as possible so we can meet the min shuffle buffer size threshold ASAP, and the shuffle operation needs to be global in terms of the shuffle buffer, so you wouldn't want to silo the data in multiple shuffle buffers on different threads. We'd still be able to apply formatting in parallel on the shuffled batches.

> For summing resources before request resources, is summing up across operators not enough? Do you mean we should sum across different executor instances that are concurrent (how this can be done)?

Yes, this is the main thing. I think a reasonably safe way to do this is to have a named actor that submits a running `sum` of requests to the autoscaler (e.g., sum of resource requests it has seen in the last 30 seconds or so). We may need to tweak the local algorithm to always submit scaling requests periodically to ensure the autoscaling.

Alternatively we could submit the running `max`, which I think would also ensure autoscaling, but may not be as good as the sum.

Ah, unfortunately this didn't work: for xterm, terminfo (at least on my macOS system) uses a more complicated escape sequence for `sgr0` (reset) `\x1b(B\x1b[m`, instead of just some variant of the normal `\x1b[0m`. `colors.strip_color` doesn't handle the `\x1b(B` escape, nor do any of the (correct) regexps in the StackOverflow discussion above. 

Instead of trying to patch holes like this or pull in a library like https://github.com/selectel/pyte to do it fully properly, I've just switched to running mypy with a simpler terminal emulator specified (`TERM=linux`). 

```shell
TERM=xterm python -c 'import curses; curses.setupterm(fd=1); print(repr(curses.tigetstr("sgr0").decode()))'
# '\x1b(B\x1b[m'

TERM=linux python -c 'import curses; curses.setupterm(fd=1); print(repr(curses.tigetstr("sgr0").decode()))'
# '\x1b[0;10m'
```

Padding issue resolved in c1f6e0018.

Each individual `part`'s `eval_set` gets padded with `None`(s) whenever there are no more parts of an individual eval_set component to distribute. So let's say that there are two eval_sets - the first one has 10 partitions and the other has 20. Then parts 11-20 for the second eval_set component will be batched up with 10 None's for padding from the first set. I can add a Lucidchart diagram in the AM.

This way, each part's `eval_set` has the same length, which should make each worker's job of consolidating eval_sets across parts immune from mixing up components' parts.

I think adding socket pointer would be helpful and make it easier for API users. 

> That said, I'm open to discussing this further if anyone feels strongly that we should be passing pointers to sockets instead of pointers to the associated context.

We should still keep `aContext` as well (all callbacks should include a `aContext` argument which should pass back what the callback provider gave when it registered the callback). 

Regarding `aContext` use in OT, it is generally tied to a callback and its meaning/use is for callback provider  (i.e. API user) to decide. 

The common use pattern is that the `aContext` is set to a pointer to the class object that is making the call into OT.  If the calling object owns/manages multiple TCP sockets, it then cannot use this simple model and need to either assign different callbacks for each socket or define a a different context struct to track the socket.

Regarding UDP socket, in my view, it would be better for it to also include the socket but we keep it as if due to legacy/compatibility reason. 

----

In particular related to the use of context here in this callback
```
typedef void (*otTcpAcceptDone)(void *aPassiveContext, void *aActiveContext, const otSockAddr *aPeer)
```
I think we want the callback definition to have a single `aContext`. Using `aPassiveContext` and `aActiveContext` this way is indirectly assuming that context are associated with some socket struct (which I don't think OT API should assume this in general case, from OT API definitions contexts are associated with a callback). I think make sense to pass the socket pointer directly here.

Further tracked it down, it's precisely about `fork` vs `spawn`. The bug does not happen if this is set to `fork`

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/9870841a126fb53a6cbab2ef7bf3d46262fa23a8/trinity/utils/mp.py#L20

I have multiple thoughts to that:

1. `fork` is more efficient as it starts with shared memory and uses copy-on-write to guarantee isolation. It is also the default on Unix. See https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/58145/how-does-copy-on-write-in-fork-handle-multiple-fork and https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2276117/python-multiprocessing-process-vs-standalone-python-vm

2. In general `spawn` provides slightly more isolation for edge cases (e.g. forking multi threaded processes may be problematic).

3. Changing the process launching strategy *because of this* feels like a sledge hammer that probably causes other (subtle) bugs so I want to continue finding an alternative fix.

The two firsts keywords are python centric. The last one is any acceptable target for a Windows shortcut. 

I agree opening a browser on the Certbot online help is not great. 

So I did another approach. The most useful action from Start Menu would be to open a command line, since it is how you interact with Certbot. So I created a batch script that do that an set it as the target for the shortcut. This script ensures that we have the administrative privileges, since it is necessary for Certbot, open the UAC if needed, then start the cmd on this elevated privileges with a little welcome echo. 

So far I think it is the most suitable thing to do.

1. Was it intentional to drop the `--non-interactive` flag, if so, why?  I would expect this is only ever used in an non-interactive context.

2. Previously, because we redirected stderr to stdout, `$OUTPUT` would contain both stdout and stderr.  Now, `$OUTPUT_FILE` will only contain output to stdout, which means we will not include stderr output in the message we post back to GitHub, which doesn't seem like what we'd want?

0 1 0

    """
    Class for legacy HP instruments from the era before SPCI, based on `pymeasure.Instrument`

    """
    status_desc = StatusBitsBase  # To be overriden by subclasses

    def __init__(self, adapter, name, status_bytes, status_bitfield, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(
            adapter, name,
            includeSCPI=False,
            **kwargs,
        )

        self.status_bytes_count = ctypes.sizeof(self.status_desc)
        self.status_bits = self.status_desc
        self.status_bytes = self.bytefield_factory(status_bytes)
        self.status_union = self.union_factory(self.status_bytes, self.status_bits)

        log.info(f"Initializing {self.name}")

    def write(self, command):
        if command == "B":
            self.adapter.connection.write("B", termination="")
        else:
            # super().write(command)
            self.adapter.connection.write(command)

    def values(self, command, **kwargs):
        if command == "B":
            self.write(command)
            return self.adapter.connection.read_raw(**kwargs)
        else:
            return super().values(command, **kwargs)

    @property
    def status(self):
        """
        Returns an object representing the current status of the unit.

        """
        # current_status = self.decode_status(self.fetch_status())
        # return current_status
        self.write("B")
        reply = self.adapter.read_bytes(self.status_bytes_count)
        return self.status_desc.from_buffer(reply).

I used `static inline` first. It can impact code-size (majorly). `inline` is recommendation to the complier. We cannot have such simple method not be inlined (the overhead of calling a function is more than the actual assembly code of the implementation).

Please see here (repeated the same in commit/PR message).
```
/**
 * This macro frees a given message buffer if not nullptr.
 *
 * This macro and the ones that follow contain small but common code patterns used in many of the core modules. They
 * are intentionally defined as macros instead of inline methods/functions to ensure that they are fully inlined.
 * Note that an `inline` method/function is not necessarily always inlined by the toolchain and not inlining such
 * small implementations can add a rather large code-size overhead.
 *
 * @param[in] aMessage    A pointer to a `Message` to free (can be nullptr).
 *
 */```

class LFPySimulator(NrnSimulator):

    """LFPy simulator"""

    def __init__(self, dt=None, cvode_active=True, cvode_minstep=None,
                 random123_globalindex=None, mechanisms_directory=None):
        """Constructor

        Args:
            dt (float): the integration time step used by neuron.
            cvode_active (bool): should neuron use the variable time step
                integration method
            cvode_minstep (float): the minimum time step allowed for a cvode
                step. Default is 0.0.
            random123_globalindex (int): used to set the global index used by
                all instances of the Random123 instances of Random
            mechanisms_directory (str): path to the parent directory of the
                directory containing the mod files. If the mod files are in
                "./data/mechanisms", then mechanisms_directory should be
                "./data/".
        """

        super(LFPySimulator, self).__init__(
            dt=dt,
            cvode_active=cvode_active,
            cvode_minstep=cvode_minstep,
            random123_globalindex=random123_globalindex,
            mechanisms_directory=mechanisms_directory
        )

    def run(
            self,
            lfpy_cell,
            lfpy_electrode,
            tstop=None,
            dt=None,
            cvode_active=None,
            random123_globalindex=None):
        """Run protocol"""
        import LFPy
        _ = self.neuron

        lfpy_cell.tstart = 0.0
        lfpy_cell.tstop = tstop

        if dt is not None:
            lfpy_cell.LFPyCell.dt = dt

        if cvode_active and dt is not None:
            raise ValueError(
                'NrnSimulator: Impossible to combine the dt argument when '
                'cvode_active is True in the NrnSimulator run method')

        if cvode_active is None:
            cvode_active = self.cvode_active

        if cvode_active is not None:
            self.cvode_active = cvode_active

        if random123_globalindex is None:
            random123_globalindex = self.random123_globalindex

        if random123_globalindex is not None:
            rng = self.neuron.h.Random()
            rng.Random123_globalindex(random123_globalindex)

        probes = [lfpy_electrode] if lfpy_electrode is not None else None

        sim_params = {
            "probes": probes,
            "rec_vmem": False,
            "rec_imem": False,
            "rec_ipas": False,
            "rec_icap": False,
            "rec_variables": [],
            "variable_dt": self.cvode_active,
            "atol": 0.001,
            "to_memory": True,
            "to_file": False,
            "file_name": None,
        }

        try:
            lfpy_cell.simulate(**sim_params)
        except Exception as e:
            raise LFPySimulatorException("LFPy simulator error", e)

        logger.debug("LFPy simulation finished")

```

What is the target supported contexts for this feature? Do we target just cpu and gpu, or we want to support other hardware backends, too?

Currently the dispatch logic is inside FCompute, which is a bit different from existing mxnet users' experience. Usually the FCompute only declares the computation, and leave the dispatch logic to MXNet executor. And it's unclear how it supports the case where the same op is extended by a library for Intel CPUs and NVIDIA GPUs - they may hard-code the dispatch logic to only care about their own hardware. How do we handle such conflicts? 

Furthermore, currently the infer_shape/infer_dtype is not context-aware, i.e. CPU and GPU infers the same dtype. However, it may not be true (e.g. cpu supports fp32 and bfloat16, and gpu supports fp32 and fp16). How do we handle these attribute conflict? 

I had a short discussion with @yzhliu and we saw two potential fixes:
1. make infer_shape/infer_dtype context aware. This way we can have different infer_dtype function for cpu & gpu. MXNet needs to dispatch to the function based on the current context. For example, `op.set_attr<FInferType>("FInferType<cpu>", my_infer_type_function)` for cpu specific type inference, and `op.set_attr<FInferType>("FInferType<gpu>", my_infer_type_function_gpu)`for gpu. 
2. Another way is to register ops with different names (e.g. 'cpu_gemm' and 'gpu_gemm'). This way they can have different infer_attr functions. But we don't want users to modify their model definition in the training script to these names. To mitigate that we can have an API to allow user to provide a mapping (e.g. {'gemm' -> 'cpu_gemm'}) for mxnet to map an op to another op registered in the backend. 

Finally, is there a plan to support dynamic custom context? :P @samskalicky

forms of) version numbers in plugin names, but that seems fine. We could
use `~`, but I think that that would be confusing (it also frequently
breaks links in PDFs written by TeX novices). We can’t use `*` without
encoding it, because it is not an unreserved character per [RFC 3986,
section 2.3][rfc]. So given that we want to permit `[A-Za-z0-9_-]`,
reserving `.` seems like the best option.

From [Microsoft's unit testing best practices](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/testing/unit-testing-best-practices#avoid-multiple-acts):

> When writing your tests, try to only include one Act per test. Common approaches to using only one act include:
> * Create a separate test for each act.
> * Use parameterized tests.

> Why?
> * When the test fails it is not clear which Act is failing.
> * Ensures the test is focussed on just a single case.
> * Gives you the entire picture as to why your tests are failing.

> Multiple Acts need to be individually Asserted and it is not guaranteed that all of the Asserts will be executed. In most unit testing frameworks, once an Assert fails in a unit test, the proceeding tests are automatically considered to be failing. This can be confusing as functionality that is actually working, will be shown as failing.

> All data processing frameworks will _mention_ their query/execution planner in their docs, since they want to emphasize optimized performance as one of their value-adds and the execution model may be relevant for debugging, setting performance expectations, etc. But that's different than requiring the user to think about their execution plan when using their APIs. The latter should only be done when required, for the sake of the typical tenets of good API design: information hiding, API should be as small/simple as possible, don't introduce unnecessary concepts, keep API concepts as high-level as possible, don't leak implementation details, etc.

I don't think just mentioning execution plan will mean performance or value added automatically -- it's just a standard thing across frameworks. Even SQL would have a statement like EXPLAIN  PLAN as part of the language for users.

The rest are just general principles of API design -- those are separate topics from whether lineage/execution plan is leaky.

> 
> This is all moot since the serialization being lineage-based seems to be a fundamental behavioral part of the API contract, so including the lineage concept in the serialization APIs seems like the best route.

Yes, exactly.

> 
> > Another option I can think of is serialize_as/to_lazy_dataset / deserialize_from_lazy_dataset
> 
> I'd push against that since this isn't correct in terms of what's supported, since lineage-based serialization is also supported for eager datasets. We track the lineage even when in eager mode, and when calling `ds.serialize_lineage()` for a dataset in eager mode, only the lineage will be serialized. The only requirement is that the dataset is **created** from external data, which we capture in the lineage as read tasks and is currently equivalent to lazy datasources.

That's why there is "as/to" in API name, so that it can apply to eager datasets as well.
That said, I'm fine with either choices, feel free to stick with serialize_lineage if you want.

A few issues here:

  - On gRPC errors, `code` and `details` are callables, not the raw
    values (note `e.code()` above). Thus, the actual exception message
    is something like

    ```
    <function grpc_error.<locals>.<lambda> at 0x7f18ea696f80>
    ```

    which is not what was wanted. Unfortunately, this case is not
    tested, so the failure is not caught.

  - Why the conditional? I was under the impression that `details()`
    always exists on an `RpcError` value. If we do need the conditional,
    it should just be `getattr(e, "details", lambda: "")()`. [The
    `hasattr` builtin is rarely what you want][hasattr].

[hasattr]: https://hynek.me/articles/hasattr/

I was thinking a bit more about this.

The original intent of the bug is two-fold:

1. Change our Papertrail logging to show INFO messages
    - Because there are a lot of code (ours and from packages) that use info by standard even though some of them should be warnings
    - Thus, such messages can be missed and it happened to me when I was try to find problems in production
2. I noticed some warning messages that should have been info messages
    - I noticed that Ionut (but anyone of us would have wanted to do the same) had some warning messages just to make them show up on Papertrail [1]
    - Only those messages should be lowered to info.

I believe we should focus on just the first point (WARNING -> INFO) and simply fix output in the future as we work on other features. We, the long-term contributors, have the context to decide which messages are clearly just informative messages versus warning ones.

I still don't get why one would want to have ~80mb of pipenv inside the container image.
Even if you run your pipeline in container one step can generate requirements.txt and the next one can pickup the file and install the dependencies.
And these steps can be in Jenkins/giltab/GitHub/your-ci or as a I mentioned multi stage docker file. 
As side note: there is some confusion always regarding the terminology.
The docker file is used to build the image .
The container is spawned from it. 
You install pipenv during the image build. And then you have it in all the containers built from it.
Pipenv is a build tool, like npm or make or GCC. IMHO it does not belong in the final container.
Whether you exclude it with my first suggestion or later is eventually the same. I chose to document the most easy one. 
I will add a note about pipenv exclusion from the final container.

Just to double check - it might also happen that we release a message while the current load is _still above 1.0_. Please confirm that this is fine, too, if it happens due to message size.

---

**An example how this could happen** (please correct me if I'm wrong):

- `FlowControl.max_messages` is set to "a lot" and `FlowControl.max_bytes` is set to 10. The user code is currently processing three messages with sizes 1, 1, and 1, respectively (load == 0.3).
- The client then receives several message with sizes 9, 8, and 7. Since the current load allows for it, we dispatch the first message (size 9). The user code is now processing messages of sizes 1, 1, 1, and 9, the load is 1.2, and two messages with sizes 8 and 7 are on hold.
- The user code ACKs a small message, that message is dropped from the lease management. The load drops to 1.1.
- Since the load went down by one message, we (incorrectly) assume that we now have capacity to release another message, thus we release the message of size 8.
- The user code is now processing messages of sizes 1, 1, 9, and 8, and the load actually jumps to 1.9.
- In unlucky cases, the issue can compound - the user code ACKs another small message (size 1), which makes the client to release another (bigger) message - size 7 this time. The user code is then processing messages of sizes 1, 9, 8, 7, and the load is 2.5 ...

Oh, this is a problem with smtlib. We free up FD 4 for the creation of a new socket, but we don't delete the backing symbolic array "socket4" from the constraint set, so when we try to create a new socket, it yells at us. Previously this was even worse, as we'd use the name "socket" for every backing array so you couldn't make more than one. 

If you want to fix it, you could do one of the following:
* Count the symbols we've created with a given name and deduplicate them by postfixing the counter
* Replace the file descriptor number in the symbol name with a long-ish random number
* Change the name of the symbol in the constraint set as soon as the FD gets closed.

Given this is likely going to be used by users with little kubernetes experience, it might be good to add `imagePullPolicy: Always` to the example. This will slightly slow down the start up in some cases, but it will still use the local node image cache. The slowdown comes from checking that the local copy's hash matches the remote image's hash.

A common issue for new kubernetes users happens when they run an image, fix a bug, push, and relaunch. If the image pull policy isn't set to always (and neither is the cluster's default), then it won't pull the newest image if the name and tag are the same. Arguably, the tag should be set to something different every time but this isn't always done.

* This will **always** evaluate to `True`:
```python
>>> ~True
-2

>>> ~False
-1
```
* The Python tilde operator is the bit-complement, so `~0 -> -` and `~1 -> -2`.
* The **NumPy** tilde operator is `not`
* In Python (and NumPy) implicit booleaness is only false for zero:
```python
>>> if ~True: print('yes')
yes

>>> if ~False: print('yes')
yes

>>> if 0: print('yes')

```

When converting from FLOAT32 to BF16 truncation can work, since the encodings line up save for the 16 lest significant bits, but that means rounding errors. I don't want to get into the exact math, instead let me use a metaphor. let's say i want to convert a 3-digit number to a 2-digit number. Further, let's say the 3-digit number has the value `1.49`. if i convert via truncation i end up with `1.4` if i convert via _rounding_ i end up with `1.5`. The `1.5` value is closer to my original value and is "more correct". The rounding scheme i chose to implement is round-to-nearest-even. Which is the most common rounding scheme. Values below 0.5 are rounded down, values above 0.5 are rounded up, and values exactly equal to 0.5 are rounded to the nearest even value.

The comment i added here around NaN relates to what happens when the value isn't a number. For infinity values the lower 16-bits are 0s, so things work fine. but for NaN the lower mantissa may contain a "NaN payload", extra information that that value is carrying that further describes it beyond just NaN. Its probably not correct to round this value (since its not a number) but it also probably isn't correct to just truncate it. If detecting NaN was easy i would have not rounded it, but because i converted to integer value first, NaN is harder to detect.

Here is the code in pytorch that implements this: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/e81bfffbe1349c5ba568a36ec911404c6e494ea0/c10/util/BFloat16.h#L51

Notice that pytorch uses round-to-nearest-even for converting from fp32 to bf16 and also it doesn't do rounding of NaN payloads.

``` python

est = Estimator(log_interval=20, train_metrics=X)

est = Estimator(log_interval='epoch', train_metrics=X)
est = Estimator(log_interval=20, train_metrics=X)

 ``` python 
est = Estimator('epoch', train_metrics=X)

* `time` - Safari
* `date` - Safari
* `datetime-local` - FF, Safari

I agreed that it is better to use as fewer dependencies as possible, but I am not sure how we can completely remove the date picker.

The `_upcast` on boxes was introduced for operators that estimate information related to a box. Things like the area for instance. If you put integer boxes and you ask for the area of a box, you kind of expecting you receive the value in integers (that's debatable but that's how the operator worked). The issue was that if you used too small of a precision, the area estimation would overflow. So this method upcasts the boxes to a space where it's safe to do multiplications without risking overflowing for most applications.

On the losses side, I'm not aware of any application that does things on integer space. Not only that but doing reduce=mean will break things. So we need to be careful to definitely not support ints in losses. Similar care might be needed for some box operators. Area might still make sense to return as integer but I'm not 100% sure if that's the case with all the IoU metrics we deal here.

So I think it's important prior merging this PR, to make an explicit decision of what has to support integers and what doesn't, handle it appropriately and add tests and xfails to ensure we are not breaking anything.

`allow_environ` only turns on whether it looks for the environment variable. If the environment variable is not there, then it acts as if you want it to use declaration files or other means. The order is:
* Look at environment variable for a mirror environment (if allow_environ is True)
* Look in config.
* Look for an environment variable for regular env and guess mirror based on that (if allow_environ is True and allow_guess is True)
* Look for regular env in config and guess mirror based on that (if allow_environ is True and allow_guess is True)

Plus you don't have to change the structure of the code if you decide adjust what the function does, whereas if you want to go from "not mock.called" to doing something slightly different, you have to write not just the changed functionality but change the test.

Refactoring of the `_MemberStatus` was unavoidable. `Tags` class dictates that child classes must implement `tags` property, but such an attribute already existed in the `_MemberStatus`. Renaming it to `_tags` wasn't possible (NamedTuple doesn't allow to have attributes that start with underscore) and therefore I just decided to keep the whole JSON response in the `data` attribute.
`tags`, `timeline`, and `watchdog_failed` attributes are returned from the JSON response as it is, and therefore I converted them to properties.
`dcs_last_seen` attribute is not used anywhere in the code. IIRC it was added to `_MemberStatus` just because it could be useful in the future. In two years it is still not used, therefore I decided to remove it. If we need it one day - adding a property would be just 4 lines of code.

`reachable`, `in_recovery`, and `wal_position` attributes are kept as it is because their values are calculated based on some logic. For example https://github.com/zalando/patroni/pull/2823/files#diff-b6a3a0ef64783a6d32feb908d74a3a009e9f25008b8ca5313c9c9747e3b74a63R54 will raise an exception is there is no `wal` or `xlog` in the JSON document and as a result the `fetch_node_status()` will return `_MemberStatus.unknown(member)`.

The condition `Get<RouterTable>().GetRouterIndex(static_cast<Router &>(*aNeighbor)) < kMaxRouters` is satisfied when `aNeighbor` references to a `Router` in the router table `mRouterTable`. 

It is all about how router table organize `Routers` in `mRouters`. **Note that the `Routers` in `mRouters` are ordered by `Router ID`.**
Let's assume that the current valid Router ID set is {1, 3, 5} and `aNeighbor` points to `Router 5`, i.e. `aNeighbor == &mRouters[2]`. If the new Router ID set is {1, 5} in the RouterTLV, after `ProcessRouteTlv`, `Router 5` is moved from `mRouters[2]` to `mRouters[1]`, however the value of `aNeighbor` is not updated accordingly. So, after `ProcessRouterTLV`, `aNeighbor` is still pointing to `&mRouters[2]`, which is actually an **INVALID** `Router`. 

This fix sets `aNeighbor = NULL` in such case so that the `aNeighbor` handling code after the exit label won't be executed.

I ended up going with an exception instead of can_read/can_write, because you mentioned in a comment that plugins should raise an exception if they can't handle a request ( https://github.com/imageio/imageio/pull/574#issuecomment-810220988 ). It follows the pythonic mantra of "better ask for forgiveness than for permission". Personally, I like the idea from an API design perspective: A plugin just handles the request, and if that fails it screams .... _loudly_.

> As it is now, every image is opened twice.

Maybe I misunderstood something, but in my mind the image file is handled by the stateful request object and will be kept open. We hand the same request to every plugin and, once bytes are read, they will remain in a memory buffer; this should be very fast to read.

> Also, let's raise a more specific error when a plugin cannot process a request. NotImplementedError seems suited, or perhaps a custom error based on it.

I was thinking about this, too, when I was writing that code. 

I first had a `NotImplementedError`, but I think doesn't match what is happening. It's not that the code to read/write/do-stuff isn't implemented - chances are it will _never_ be implemented -, but rather that the request can not be handled (e.g. we are trying to open a `.mp4` using `pillow`). Essentially, the input value passed to the constructor function is not valid, so I ended up choosing `ValueError`. I'm happy to raise a different error though if you think it makes more sense.

I have a personal thing against custom errors. I've never used them in a real project, and when I've encountered them they could have likely been refactored into a standard error + a good error string/message. We can introduce them, but I don't really understand how they add anything over `StandardError("This is what went wrong.")`.

FWIW, I find reading the terse forms is a big speedbump.  The terse form is confusing, because it discards the precision bit, although it works for just ripping off the base type:

```python
>>> import re
>>> terse = r"(\w+)\(\s*\d+\s*(,\s*\d+\s*)*\)"
>>> re_terse = re.compile(terse)
>>> re_terse.match("NUMERIC(1)").groups()
('NUMERIC', None)
>>> re_terse.match("NUMERIC(1,3)").groups()
('NUMERIC', ',3')
```

The verbose form with the same behavior:

```python
>>> verbose = r"""
...     (\w+)          # base type
...     \(             # literal open paren
...     \s*            # whitespace
...     \d+            # length / precision
...     \s*            # whitespace
...     (              # optional scale group
...     ,              # literal comma
...     \s*            # whitespace
...     \d+            # scale
...     \s*            # whitespace
...     )*             # close scale group
...     \)             # literal close paren
... """
>>> re_verbose = re.compile(verbose, re.VERBOSE)
>>> re_verbose.match("NUMERIC(1)").groups()
('NUMERIC', None)
>>> re_verbose.match("NUMERIC(1 ,3)").groups()
('NUMERIC', ',3')
```

For future readability, I'd be inclined to have compiled versions for each of the main cases (`STRING`, `BYTES`, `{,BIG}NUMERIC`) which gave back named groups, although I realize that using them in this particular case would be trickier.

    # From opencensus.trace.tracer.Tracer#should_sample
    def should_sample(self):

        Else follow the decision of the sampler.

        :rtype: bool
        :returns: Whether to trace the request or not.
        """
        return self.span_context.trace_options.enabled \
            or self.sampler.should_sample(self.span_context.trace_id)

The `cached_messages_pending` is set from an additional bit status flag I added to the cache coordinator (`cached_in_queue`) that indicates if any worker has a cached tensor pending (see line here: https://github.com/horovod/horovod/pull/902/files#diff-98391bc0c5b3bf87519e745b4ec3b9fdR796). So in the case that no tensors are reduced for a while, this flag is false and the stall message logic isn't triggered (and the `last_perform_operation` time point is updated so that once another cache messaged shows up, the stall warning isn't immediately triggered).

I considered keep track of how long the `cached_messages_pending` flag remains true and if that exceeds a threshold, trigger the stall message. The issue with this though is that this flag does cannot discern stale tensors, so in the case that you have 60 seconds of continuous cycles with tensors to reduce, it would view that as a stall condition. This is why I based the stall timing on the time since the last perform operation.

The basic idea of supporting nesting is that we don't have to check if the spinner is hidden before we use it in a `with` statement. E.g. the spinner might be used in a bigger cli application, where it is passed around to different helper methods. The helper methods might not know the current state of the spinner (another helper method might already have hidden the spinner), but might want to make sure that the spinner is hidden for their use-case. If we don't support nesting then a nested `with` might unintentionally unhide the spinner on leave.

What's the failure you're observing? rustfmt is still running?

I think there's two levels of 'skip':

1. global "never run rustfmt" via `--rustfmt-skip`, which sets `RustfmtSubsystem.skip`
2. local "don't run rustfmt on one crate" via `rust_crate(name="crate", skip_rustfmt=True)` in a `BUILD` file, which sets `SkipRustfmtField` for that crate

I think you've correctly implemented 2, but this test is for 1. So, there's potentially two steps to do to resolve this:

1. add support for the global `skip` flag. For example, what black does: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/75b7be14501ff3228ef1eae4e8a413c371564913/src/python/pants/backend/python/lint/black/rules.py#L68-L73 (I note that this typically happens within an explicit partitioner, which this subsystem doesn't currently use. I'm not sure what the correct path forward there is, though.)
2. add a test for the per-crate `skip_rustfmt=True` flag

The reason this method can return None (iirc) is that if you don't return a level, the workunit completes with the level it started with... ie, whatever the `@rule` is marked with. So I don't think it makes sense to always return a non-None value here, as that will override your `@rule(level=..)`... and maybe result in something logging that it is `[INFO] Starting` and then `[DEBUG] Completed`... which would be very odd.

So it feels like this should be:
```
def level(self) -> Optional[LogLevel]:
    return LogLevel.WARN if self.exit_code != 0 else None
```

I imagine you're aware of the comparable PEX functionality... In that case, the preamble _has_ to be Python, and the bootstrap script includes the code before anything else: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/b51b340bea8a585a246997071a815d008986d6e3/pex/pex_builder.py#L447-L450

For our specific needs, we want to modify the entry point at runtime if the script is invoked with args. With PEX, we update `os.environ`. As this is currently implemented, the environment is already set up by the time the preamble script runs, so we'd instead need to modify `env` directly.

So, whichever solution you land on, we hope it supports modifying the environment so that we can dynamically set the entry point at runtime 🤞

```

Old-style format, still supported in Python3:
```
            title = '%s %s' % (title, part, )

```
            title = '%(title)s %(part)s' % {'title': title, 'part': part, }
```
```
            # if title and part are local vars ...
            title = '%(title)s %(part)s' % locals()
```
Newer-style, but still OK in 2.
```
            title = '{0} {1}'.format(title, part)
```
```
            title = '{title} {part}'.format(title=title, part=part)
```

Let's mimic a `DatabaseError` in a simple way.

### What's in crud

#### For non-many
We have `res, err`, where `err` is a single error table from `errors` rock and `res` is `nil`.

#### For many
We have `res, err`, where `err` is an array of table errors and `res` is common result like in plain return.

### What's in python

`DatabaseError` is an `Exception` with `code`, `message` and `extra_info` (the same error, but with additional fields based on provided `box.error` info).

### How we can mix it

First case is rather simple. We can raise some error with the same code and message we've got from Tarantool. But since we've got additional info, it would be nice to provide it.

Second case is much complicated. My proposal is as follows:

- Have two different classes for non-many and many errors (`CrudModuleError` and `CrudModuleManyError`).
- Both should inherit `DatabaseError`.

For `CrudModuleError`, let's add the `extra_info_errors` attribute with the original error (`DatabaseError` has `extra_info` field with similar meaning, but different structure).

For `CrudModuleManyError`, let's raise error based on `DatabaseError(0, "Got multiple errors, see errors_list")`, store original errors list in `errors_list` attribute and success tuples in `success_tuples` attribute (I'm not insist on naming here -- it's just an example, but I think the current `res`, `errs` one is a bit confusing).

You also may define a new class for `errors` Lua rock errors since they always have the same structure, but this is optional -- dict is fine too.

@feuGeneA  I was looking at this and thought there might be a logic issue here...
```
>>> from web3._utils import abi
>>> tuple = '(int,(address,address),(address,address),int)'
>>> abi.get_tuple_component_types(tuple)
['int', '(address,address),(address,address)', 'int']
```
instead of
```
['int', '(address,address)','(address,address)', 'int']
```
Since this does a reverse lookup of the closing paren, it can't handle a tuple with multiple tuples. This logic should fix that:
```
def get_tuple_component_types(tuple_type):
    components = tuple_type[1:-1]
    
    if '(' not in components and ')' not in components:
        # There were no tuples in the list, so we can just split on commas
        return components.split(',')
    
    split_components = re.split('([\.^(^)])', components)    
    reduced_components = list(filter(lambda val: val not in ['', ',', '(', ')'], split_components))
    clean_components = list(map(lambda val: val.strip(','), reduced_components))
    return list(map(lambda maybeTup: '(' + maybeTup + ')' if ',' in maybeTup else maybeTup, clean_components)) 
```
This works on the following cases:
```
(int,int) # Tuple with no inner tuples
['int', 'int']

(int,(address,address),(address,address),int) # Tuple with consecutive inner tuples
['int', '(address,address)', '(address,address)', 'int']

((address,address),int,(address,address),int,(address,address)) # Tuple with tuples as the first and last types
['(address,address)', 'int', '(address,address)', 'int', '(address,address)']
```
this still falls short in the event that we have inner nested tuples:
```
(int,(address,(int,int)),int) # Tuple with nested inner tuple
['int', 'address', '(int,int)', 'int']
```
and may be inefficient due to the creation of so many lists, but I think it's a good starting point / a step closer.

The scopes are set at initialization, and changing them afterwords would require changing private state (one or both of _scopes or _downscope). I don't see any way to get around storing that state either, since the refresh function of the new credentials object would need to be aware that it needs to request reduced scopes. It needs to ask for reduced scope every time, since access token request scope defaults to the full scope granted by the refresh token (https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-6). I can't think of any way to implement a downscope function that doesn't circle back around to creating an object that has basically the same implementation as the one proposed here.

Side Note: The credentials as written could already be considered to have mutable scopes, since (per https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-5.1) the server is not required to return an access token with all the scopes of the refresh token.

* The `fetch_schema` attribute in the connection object should not be available to the user, it should be made private (now it is possible to change the parameter like `conn.fetch_schema=...` for an existing `conn` object, now `fetch_schema` is public). We want the `fetch_schema` parameter is manually set by user only when establishing a connection.
    + If it is decided to make the `fetch_schema` private, then we can implement a getter (`conn.is_fetch_schema_enable() -> Bool`), so we can understand the type of connection.
* However, the connection can be made from `schema-less` to `schema-full`. To do this, it is enough for the user to call `conn.update_schema(conn.schema_version)`. After that, all methods that were previously unavailable for `schema-less` connection become available. At the same time, the scheme starts updating automatically. And `conn.is_fetch_schema_enable()` will start returning `True`.
* It is not possible to change the connection type from `schema-full` to `schema-less`.

I think `Processed` should be retriable. Imagine Node A sent Node B a `LockExpired` message, which got processed by node B and then the message `Processed` was sent by Node B. Assuming that the `Processed` message was never received by Node A, this will trigger Node A to re-send the lock-expired, which will lead to Node B rejecting that message because it already processed it, but Node A doesn't know that the `LockExpired` message was processed because the `Processed` message was never received. Therefore, i think it should be retried to notify the receiving party.

Doesn't belong to this PR, but I feel, `extend_embedder_vocab` API should have been just

```python
extend_embedder_vocab(self, embedding_sources_mapping: Dict[str, str] = None):
```
Passing `extended_vocab` isn't necessary and potentially unsafe. We only should allow vocab that's for sure extended from original model.vocab. Allowing to pass arbitrary `extended_vocab` can put user in weird issues if one tries to use it API as standalone and doesn't know that extended_vocab must be extended from original model.vocab.

Since vocab extension is anyway happening in-place, following should work just fine:

```python
model.vocab.extend_from_instances(instances)
model.extend_embedder_vocab(embedding_sources_mapping)
```

And is cleaner+safer than current:
```python
model.vocab.extend_from_instances(instances)
model.extend_embedder_vocab(model.vocab, embedding_sources_mapping)
```

Looks like you're missing a leading `:` when you concatenate DEFAULT_CIPHERS and "HIGH:!DH:!aNULL".
You're appending "HIGH" to the last cipher spec from the defaults, so you're messing up the last cipherstring in the list:

```py
>>> (DEFAULT_CIPHERS + "HIGH:!DH:!aNULL")[-20:]
':!MD5HIGH:!DH:!aNULL'
>>> # !MD5HIGH is not a valid spec, so you're invalidating the original !MD5
```

It still works because the only thing really needed for AFIP at the moment is to block Diffie–Hellman because of the weak DH group they have configured. This means `:!DH` is all you need, I think. Also `!aNULL` is already in the defaults, and `HIGH` is kind of redundant, because you're appending to the defaults, not creating an independent cipherlist.

```py
CIPHERS = DEFAULT_CIPHERS + ":!DH"
```

When we import a block, we need to determine the signer of the block so that we know where to send the tx fees. Holding on to this information is the job of the `ExecutionContextAPI`. However, to retrieve the signer we need to have access to the full header that is being imported.

I considered it briefly, here is my thought process:
- We could use a CLI arg parsing library, like `clap`. However, that would involve re-implementing the JVM CLI specification in that library. Most of these libs are opinionated (like `scopt` with one-dash options), so that might be more trouble than it's worth.

- We _could_ use a parsing library, as we really just want to parse the context-free grammar:
```
CLI => jvm_args* classpath jvm_args* main_class client_args
jvm_args => r"-[^\w]+"
classpath => ("-cp"|"-classpath") "[^-][^\w*]"
main_class => "[^-][^\w*]"
client_args => "[@,-][^\w]+"
``` 
This looks like any LR1 parser could handle it. However, when I looked into parsing libs for Rust, the options were not very compelling: Either use an antlr-like "your grammar is defined in a DSL in a separate file" lib, or `nom`, which is a great parser combinator lib, but it's geared towards byte-level operations.
In both cases, it seemed easier to implement the parsing by hand for our case.

However, I might have missed something, definitely open to suggestions. In particular, if there is any lib that implements the spec for JVM CLIs.

My reasoning for implementing it this way was to make things in a sense 'just work'.

If a developer A writes a plugin that allows them to publish a file to some form of file store, then developer B writes a plugin that bundles some files together, both should work together for the end-user.

Having a specific field type for each type of publishable target would mean that either A would need to know about B to add support, or the end user would have to write their own plugin to shim A and B together.

With this implementation, A just needs to ensure they do some sanity-checking on input, and B just needs to ensure their target has a `publish_targets` field.

Yes, there are cases where things are not compatible. i.e. uploading a docker image (a 'fileless' artifact) to a file-store. Or a tarball to a pypy repository. But to me, it does not seem too egregious to just error with a "I can't do that".

For one: Streams are not really hashable (I'm surprised that it allowed it as the key for a dict) -- even if we keep it, it should return an empty dict if nothing is found.  There's something that can bite you about:

```
# simplify durations:
for el in s.recurse():
     didSplit = el.splitAtDurations()
     if didSplit:
         print(didSplit[0])  # Bang! dies on a nested stream.
```

IDEs and typecheckers won't know why el.splitAtDurations() returned a Dict this time instead of a List.

Maybe we could have a `returnChangedElements=False` keyword where if someone specifically opts in to this, it returns something more useful.  But that should be List[Tuple[Stream, List[Music21Object]]] to avoid the hashing on Streams.  So then you can do:

```
for changedStream, changedElements in s.splitAtDurations(recurse=True, returnChangedElements=True):
     print(f'{changedStream}: {changedElements}')
```

Okay so there are two different scenarios and I think we want to handle them differently:

1. The original time was not recorded. As explained above, this _should_ never happen because local execution should always have the timing recorded, and `cache.rs` and `remote_cache.rs` solely work with local processes, not remote caching. We're only handling this case for type safety - if you want, we could error or use `Result` in this case, so that we eagerly know when the original process time wasn't recorded? I'm not comfortable with using 0 to represent this case.
2. The cache hit was slower than the original process, but still faster than rerunning the process (via speculation). This is the negative numbers case. We, by definition, cannot know how slow the process would be on the second run, so the best we can do is represent with a 0. That sounds sensible to me.

So, we could change the modeling of `.time_saved_from_cache()` be `Result<Duration, String>`, where a Duration of 0 means the cache hit was slower than original process. Or stick with `Option<String>` and use 0. Wdyt?

> also the matrix creation is a bit cumbersome

I can help! You can use `data.matrix()` in R to extract the data from a `data.frame` into an R matrix object.

I'd do it like this:

```r
raw_df <- data.frame(
  all_random = runif(100L)
  , two_vals = rep(c(1L, 2L), 50L)
  , three_vals = c(rep(c(0L, 1L, 2L), 33L), 0L)
  , two_vals_plus_missing = c(rep(c(1L, 2L), 49L), NA_real_, NA_real_)
  , all_zero = rep(0L, 100L)
)
raw_mat <- data.matrix(raw_df)
```

Adding column names in the call to `data.frame()` isn't necessary, but I do think it's nice to have because it makes it a bit clearer what the intention of each column is.

> because in the end the matrix is numeric, not integer

An R matrix object is a multi-dimensional version of the R vector object. Both have the constraint that all data must be the same type.

So when you call `matrix()` or `as.matrix()` or `data.matrix()`, the resulting object will have all values of the same type, and R will do casting to get them all to the same common type. Given a mix of numeric and integer columns, the resulting matrix will be all numeric.

You can confirm that by checking the "storage mode" of the matrix.

```r
df <- data.frame(int_col = seq_len(10L), numeric_col = rnorm(10L))
sapply(df, class)
#    int_col numeric_col
#  "integer"   "numeric"

dmat <- data.matrix(df)
storage.mode(dmat)
# [1] "double"
```

In case a user presents an all-integer matrix, the LightGBM R package converts it to double before passing it to the C++ code used for Dataset construction:

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/1d8bfd8c9b3fa7d56db81201adedb61cfc11669c/R-package/R/lgb.Dataset.R#L80-L85

The difficulties in this process explain why #4323 would be really valuable to have.

I wonder whether we need an explicit margin here. The 1s margin in `if expires_on > int(time.time())` seems okay to me. My reasoning:
- functionally, this line served to hardcode `token_refresh_offset=300`
  - if all cached tokens would expire within 300 seconds, this method would return `None`, prompting the caller to acquire a new token
- `token_refresh_offset` will now be observed by callers of this method
- when a caller enters its refresh window, it should begin trying to acquire a new token
- while trying to acquire a new token, the caller should return any valid token it has

One bad outcome that could follow is the caller using a token that expires in flight. That request will fail, but the caller's other option was to raise without sending the request at all, because it couldn't acquire a new token. It seems better to try the request, which could after all succeed.

What do you think?

>>> df        # df without reindexing the value would be like:
   a  b  c    #    a  b  c

Your use case specifically mention full or unhealthy disks as the reason for wanting fallback PEX_ROOT values. This test tests neither of those things. The implication of this PR is that you manually - or via some automation on your nodes - change filesystem permissions on disks that have been determined to disk full or have gone unhealthy. My expecatation was that you'd be changing the test here to actually attempt a mkdir to detect disk full.

If you are in fact doing the permission change on your nodes then you could just be using a PEX rc file on those nodes and changing that file too. This [documentation ](https://pex.readthedocs.io/en/v2.1.112/buildingpex.html?highlight=pexrc#tailoring-pex-execution-at-build-time) is only partly correct but the CLI help, which should always be your primary reading material, is accurate:
```
$ pex --help | grep -A5 pexrc
  --rcfile RC_FILE      An additional path to a pexrc file to read during
                        configuration parsing, in addition to reading
                        `/etc/pexrc` and `~/.pexrc`. If
                        `PEX_IGNORE_RCFILES=true`, then all rc files will be
                        ignored. (default: None)
```

So you could just drop in an `/etc/pexrc ` ith contents:
```
PEX_ROOT=/disk1/.pex
# PEX_ROOT=/disk2/.pex
```
And edit the file when `/disk1` fails.

I wonder if we should still have `try/finally` here, since if we end up with an error in any of the transformations when we could end up leaking memory.

The only issues I potentially see are data that aren't actually bytes, or is `size` is wrong.

```python
try:
    return DDWaf_result(...)
finally:
    ddwaf_result_free(ctypes.byref(result))
    ddwaf_context_destroy(ctx)
```


In fact, `ctx` could leak as well, if there is an error anywhere between L116 and the end of the function.

maybe better as:


```python
ctx = ddwaf_context_init(self._handle)
try:
    result = ddwaf_result()
    try:
        return DDWaf_result(...)
    finally:
        ddwaf_result_free(ctypes.byref(result))
finally:
    ddwaf_context_destroy(ctx)
```

@rhcarvalho @untitaker In this particular test case as well, there is no events data which was coming and thus only comparison was with log_result that we get from response.
Now, I've figured out why this event data is not coming as well. It is because in the initialization error wrapper that we have currently written, it takes the execution info directly from the system (sys.exc_info) and not from an exact exception which is generated. So, we actually need to generate an exception as we only receive data from (sys.exc_info) in the post_init_error method and not the actual exception. A simple solution is reraise the exception from the (sys.exc_info) and then capture that exception information, which solves all the problems. I've tested and verified it. If we use the default pickling mechanism, then `pickle.dumps` function works, but `pickle.loads` function does not (there is `RecursionError`). This error occurs because there is an attempt to call the `__setstate__` method, the call to this method goes through `__getattr__`, in which there is a check to see if the data is materialized, this check uses the `self._value` attribute. However, at this moment, the object is not yet ready be used, `self.__dict__` attribute is empty, which means that the access to `self._value` attribute will also go through `__getattr__` function. 
It turns out something like this: `getattr(ModinIndex, "__setstate__")` -> `ModinIndex.__getattr__` -> `ModinIndex.is_materialized` ->  `ModinIndex.__getattr__` -> `ModinIndex.is_materialized` -> and so on.

When we define our serialization function (`__reduce__`), we return a callback for how to build an object that doesn't have the same problems as the default mechanism.

The generated class is roughly equivalent to,

```
class MyEnum(object):
    ITEM_1 = 1
    ITEM_2 = 2
```

So `__hash__` on the object is never called because `MyEnum()` is never used.

That said, I suppose there's no harm in keeping this.

It is still possible in a small selection of cases.

1. `errors` is initialized to `[]`. If the body isn't JSON or is the wrong top-level shape (e.g., top-level array), `errors` will stay `[]`
2. A Type Zero or undefined format can produce an errors array of `[]` if it explicitly sets`"errors": []`.

Regarding (2), we talked about this and I was waffling about it. But in another conversation, we looked again at the draft error spec and the current wording there is as follows:

```
Clients MAY ignore any `"errors"` key in Type Zero Globus Errors and always set `errors` to `[]`.

If the error payload has a top-level key `"errors"` containing an array, and every element of that array is an object, then errors on the parsed response MAY be populated with the `"errors"` in the response.

Absent an `"errors"` key, clients MAY populate `errors` with the root response object.

Clients MUST NOT populate the `errors` of a Parsed Globus Error from a Type Zero Globus Error with data which is not here specified as a valid parse.
```

Now, obviously we're talking about a draft spec that we're working on contemporaneously with this first implementation. So we could change the wording.

But as specified here, the spec was written to say that `errors=[<root_doc>]` is valid only if `"errors"` was not present as a key. If it is present, our choices are:
- respect any well-formed `errors` data (to the above definition of well-formed-ness)
- set `errors=[]`

I'm not sure it's convincing and perfect but it makes some sense in that an API which says `errors=[]` has explicitly told us that there are no sub-errors.

class Keysight34980A34980A:
    def __init__(self):
        self.ch = ChannelManager()


class ChannelManager:
    def __init__(self):
        self.channel = 0

    def __call__(self, channel):
        self.channel = channel
        return self

    def do_something(self):
        print(f"doing something to channel {self.channel}")


a = Keysight34980A34980A()
a.ch(1).do_something()
a.ch(2).do_something()

# Calling without the number will use the last entered channel number
a.ch.do_something()

# You can't just store a channel, because you constantly refer to the same object
ch1 = a.ch(1)
ch2 = a.ch(2)
ch1.do_something()
# but you can using copy if you'd want
import copy
ch1 = copy.copy(a.ch(1))
ch2 = copy.copy(a.ch(2))
ch1.do_something()
ch2.do_something()
```

On further thought, this line changes behavior, so please delete it. This applies to both `Task` and `WatchdogTask`.

Explanation:

With the prior release, calling `close` twice prints a warning:
```
>>> import nidaqmx
>>> task = nidaqmx.Task()
>>> task.close()
>>> task.close()
D:\dev\measurementlink-python\examples\nidaqmx_analog_input\.venv\lib\site-packages\nidaqmx\task.py:456: ResourceWarning: Attempted to close NI-DAQmx task of name "_unnamedTask<0>" but task was already closed.
  warnings.warn(
```

It also prints a warning if the context manager calls `close` the 2nd time:
```
>>> with nidaqmx.Task() as task:
...     task.close()
...
D:\dev\measurementlink-python\examples\nidaqmx_analog_input\.venv\lib\site-packages\nidaqmx\task.py:456: ResourceWarning: Attempted to close NI-DAQmx task of name "_unnamedTask<1>" but task was already closed.
  warnings.warn(
```

If `close` clears `self._close_on_exit`, then the 2nd snippet will no longer print a warning. This allows users to double-close the task when using a context manager.

Hypothetical: If we wanted to allow multiple calls to `close`, the right way to do it would be to remove the warning.

Meh... when I woke up, I realized that the negative lookbehind fails if there  is a _triple_ precent sign in the string, because that preceding percent is actually escaped by a yet another percent and does not affect the percent that starts our substitution placeholder.

The following test case fails:
```py
(
    "values(%%%(foo:INT64)s, %(bar)s)",
    ("values(%%%(foo)s, %(bar)s)", dict(foo="INT64")),
)
```

The negative lookbehind should actually match an odd number of percent characters before the actual `%(...)` pattern (because if there's even number of them, they do not escape the starting `%` in the potential match).

An even number of `%`s can be matched with the following:
```
[^%]*((%%)+[^%]*)+
```
but that can't be used as a lookbehind, because it's not fixed width, and I'm starting to dislike this rabbit hole. :rabbit: :hole: (is the mini-formatting language even regular?)

We might be better-off if we simply catch all preceding `%` chars and manually check their length in the processing cod. The following worked for me, although I did not test it that thoroughly, and mostly relied on the existing test cases:

```patch
diff --git google/cloud/bigquery/dbapi/cursor.py google/cloud/bigquery/dbapi/cursor.py
index f4ff0a3..1d1fc7f 100644
--- google/cloud/bigquery/dbapi/cursor.py
+++ google/cloud/bigquery/dbapi/cursor.py
@@ -479,7 +479,7 @@ def _format_operation(operation, parameters):
 
 
 def _extract_types(
-    operation, extra_type_sub=re.compile(r"(?<!%)%(?:\(([^:)]*)(?::(\w+))?\))?s").sub,
+    operation, extra_type_sub=re.compile(r"(%*)%(?:\(([^:)]*)(?::(\w+))?\))?s").sub,
 ):
     """Remove type information from parameter placeholders.
 
@@ -492,7 +492,13 @@ def _extract_types(
 
     def repl(m):
         nonlocal parameter_types
-        name, type_ = m.groups()
+        percent_prefix, name, type_ = m.groups()
+        if len(percent_prefix) % 2:
+            # An odd number of % chars precede the match, meaning that the
+            # leading percent in %(...) is actually escaped and should not be treated
+            # as a replacement placeholder.
+            return m.group()
+
         try:
             if name:
                 if not parameter_types:
@@ -510,7 +516,7 @@ def _extract_types(
                     if not isinstance(parameter_types, dict):
                         raise TypeError()
 
-                return f"%({name})s"
+                return f"{percent_prefix}%({name})s"
             else:
                 if parameter_types is None:
                     parameter_types = []
diff --git tests/unit/test_dbapi_cursor.py tests/unit/test_dbapi_cursor.py
index 9f25a24..5afe269 100644
--- tests/unit/test_dbapi_cursor.py
+++ tests/unit/test_dbapi_cursor.py
@@ -787,6 +787,18 @@ class TestCursor(unittest.TestCase):
         ),
         (" %s %()s %(:int64)s ", (" %s %s %s ", [None, None, "int64"])),
         (" %%s %s %()s %(:int64)s ", (" %%s %s %s %s ", [None, None, "int64"])),
+        (
+            "values(%%%(foo:INT64)s, %(bar)s)",
+            ("values(%%%(foo)s, %(bar)s)", dict(foo="INT64")),
+        ),
+        (
+            "values(%%%%(foo:INT64)s, %(bar)s)",
+            ("values(%%%%(foo:INT64)s, %(bar)s)", dict()),
+        ),
+        (
+            "values(%%%%%(foo:INT64)s, %(bar)s)",
+            ("values(%%%%%(foo)s, %(bar)s)", dict(foo="INT64")),
+        ),
     ],
 )
 def test__extract_types(inp, expect):
```

Ah right, good question, I'm hardwired to quoting everything. The quotes are not necessary unless you want to have the separator in the string itself, consider the following:

```
    :hide_key: /$defs/air, earth, fire, and water/
```

`air, earth, fire, and water` is a valid JSON key, but if we did split() with `,` separator we'd parse it as 4 different paths. The csv parser takes care of that - it can deal with both unquoted and quoted input.
